prompt: What is Walrus and how does it work?
expected_output: Explains Walrus as a decentralized blob storage protocol on Sui with erasure coding, three-layer architecture, and public-by-default data.
expectations: Describes Walrus as a decentralized storage protocol built on Sui
Mentions erasure coding (RedStuff) for distributing data across storage nodes
Explains the three layers: client, storage nodes, Sui blockchain
States that all blobs are public by default
Mentions the Point of Availability (PoA) concept

prompt: What is the difference between a blob ID and a Sui object ID in Walrus?
expected_output: Blob ID is a content hash (URL-safe base64) for reading. Object ID is a Sui hex ID for lifecycle operations. Same content = same blob ID but different object IDs per upload.
expectations: Explains blob ID is content-addressed (derived from blob content)
Explains object ID is a Sui on-chain identifier (0x... hex)
States that same content produces the same blob ID
States that each upload creates a different object ID even for identical content
Clarifies blob ID is for reading, object ID is for lifecycle operations (extend, delete, share)

prompt: I want to store files on Walrus from my web app. Should I use the CLI, HTTP API, or TypeScript SDK?
expected_output: For a web app, use the TypeScript SDK with an upload relay for browser uploads. HTTP API is simpler for server-side. CLI is for terminal/scripting.
expectations: Recommends the TypeScript SDK for web app integration
Mentions the upload relay for browser-based uploads
Mentions the HTTP API as an alternative for server-side code
Mentions the CLI for terminal/scripting use cases
Does not recommend the CLI as the primary choice for a web app
